text:
Good investing outcomes come from doing a few boring things well.

For most people, investing boils down to a handful of things:
- Decide on the right asset allocation for your financial goal and time horizon. 
- Stick to low-cost index funds or ETFs instead of trying to pick stocks.
- Invest regularly, come rain or shine.
- Increase the amount you invest as your income grows.
- Rebalance your portfolio periodically.
- Stay invested.

That's really it.

The problem is that while this sounds simple, it isn't easy.

How much equity should you own? How much debt? Should you have some gold? Which funds should you pick? How often should you rebalance? And as your goals get closer, should your asset allocation change?

These are genuine questions, and they're not always easy to answer.

To make matters worse, there are thousands of mutual funds and ETFs to choose from. Even if you've already decided to stick to low-cost index funds, the sheer number of choices is maddening.

This complexity is one reason many people continue to park money in fixed deposits or insurance products, even when they know there may be better alternatives. Investing often feels intimidating before it even begins.

This is exactly the problem that lifecycle funds (or target-date funds, as they're called in some countries) are designed to solve.

You pick a fund that roughly matches the horizon of your financial goal. The fund takes care of everything else: choosing the asset allocation, rebalancing the portfolio, and gradually reducing equity exposure while increasing debt exposure as you move closer to your goal.

The logic is simple. The farther away you are from your goal, the more risk you can afford to take. The closer you get, the more important it becomes to preserve your capital. If you're retiring in a few years, for example, having your entire retirement corpus in equities probably doesn't make much sense because equity markets can be highly volatile over short periods.

Until recently, this category didn't exist in India. SEBI has now introduced lifecycle funds, and through @ZerodhaAMC, we're the first AMC to launch them.

The underlying portfolio is intentionally simple and low-cost. The equity allocation is through the Zerodha Nifty LargeMid 250 Index Fund, giving you exposure to India's top 250 large- and mid-cap companies. The debt allocation is through government securities, with allocations to gold and silver for diversification and arbitrage for tax efficiency.

If you're looking for a simple, low-maintenance way to invest towards a long-term goal, this is about as close as you can get to a one-fund solution.

The NFOs for our lifecycle funds are now open. Check out the link below to learn more.

text:
Zerodha's Next Growth Engine?  

Zerodha has applied for a Category-I Merchant Banking license, marking its entry into IPOs, M&A advisory, and corporate finance  The move diversifies revenue away from brokerage, which faces pressure from tighter SEBI regulations and softer trading activity.  

With a large retail client base and strong technology platform, Zerodha could build an integrated ecosystem spanning broking, AMC, lending, and investment banking.  

If approved, this could transform Zerodha from a discount broker into a full-stack capital markets powerhouse.  

What investors should watch 
=> Approval of the SEBI license. 
=> Whether Zerodha focuses initially on SME IPOs or directly targets mainboard IPOs. 
=> Ability to attract experienced investment bankers. => Success in winning IPO mandates against established incumbents. 
=> Cross-selling opportunities with its brokerage and AMC businesses.  

Zerodha is positioning itself higher up the capital markets value chain. If executed well, merchant banking could become a meaningful fee-based growth engine and make the company a far more diversified financial services franchise over the next 5–10 years.

Follow @DhawalDoshi5 for more updates.

query: ((zerodha OR groww OR dhan OR upstox OR angelone OR "discount broker") (nifty OR option OR fno OR trading OR brokerage)) min_faves:10 lang:en since:2026-06-22 until:2026-06-30
text:
Zerodha Applies for Merchant Banking Licence, Eyes Entry into Investment Banking

Zerodha has applied to SEBI for a Category-I Merchant Banking licence, marking its planned entry into the investment banking business

The proposed business will focus on IPOs, corporate fundraising and issue-related advisory services

The application was filed by Zerodha Corporate Advisors Private Limited in April and is currently under SEBI's review

The company said it will share detailed business plans after receiving regulatory approval

Zerodha joins a growing list of firms seeking merchant banking licences, with around 13 applications currently under SEBI's consideration

The move comes as India's IPO market is reviving, with companies raising over ₹19,800 crore through 20 IPOs till May, compared with 103 IPOs raising ₹1.76 lakh crore in 2025

Over the past few years, Zerodha has diversified beyond broking into asset management, lending through Zerodha Capital, proprietary investments through Rainmatter, and international investing via GIFT City

The company has also secured a broker-dealer licence from IFSCA to facilitate overseas investing through GIFT City

Zerodha's earlier application for a payments bank licence with the RBI did not materialize

The diversification comes amid tighter regulations affecting brokerage revenues, including changes in client float income and derivatives trading

As of April 2026, Zerodha had 684,700 active clients, making it the second-largest broker in India after Groww

Zerodha's FY25 revenue stood at ₹8,847 crore, compared with ₹9,993 crore in FY24, highlighting the company's focus on building new revenue streams beyond its core broking business

Merchant banking will enable Zerodha to participate in IPO management, mergers & acquisitions, corporate fundraising, valuations and underwriting, expanding its presence across the capital markets ecosystem

#Oil has sold off hugely since the MOU between the #US and #Iran. It hasn't quite filled the gap at $67.67 but has come very close. That gap was the one caused when the war started so closing that would take oil right back to pre-war levels.

So for all these reason, I remain long and looking for a move back to $80.

text:
Yes, I went for the interview without researching the company or the founders. The job description mentioned that both founders were IITians and that the company had funding, so I assumed it must be a decent place.

However, the interview location was a residential building in Royal Palms, Goregaon. That was a red flag, but I was still okay with it because many small companies in Mumbai operate out of residential buildings that have been converted into offices.

I’m a very punctual person in real life. I always leave an hour early to account for any delays caused by traffic or rain. Since it was the rainy season, I decided not to take my bike and opted for an auto instead. Most autos refused to go into the inner parts of Aarey, but one kind auto driver finally dropped me at a point from where I could take a shared auto.

When I reached the building, it gave me a slightly spooky vibe. Despite it being a huge township, there were very few people around. I still tried to stay positive. I took the lift to the 16th or 17th floor. After getting out, I felt even more skeptical. Almost every flat on that floor was locked, and the passage was dimly lit.

For a few seconds, I thought about just leaving. But then I told myself that I had already come this far, so I should at least attend the interview for the experience. With these mixed thoughts, I rang the doorbell. Someone opened the door within seconds and asked me to sit inside.

I noticed that only the two founders were present. As I looked around, I saw that the rest of the flat was completely empty except for the living room, which had just three or four Nilkamal plastic chairs and a plastic table. On the table were two laptops, some snacks, and water bottles. That was it.

At that point, I realised I shouldn’t have come. One of the founders then told me that they had invited more than 15 candidates, but I was the only one who showed up. He even said I could leave if I wanted to. Just to be polite, I said I would continue with the interview.

Before starting, both founders introduced themselves. They were IIT Roorkee graduates who had worked at Barclays for over a decade. Now they were building an algo trading software product that they planned to sell. I was actually impressed because it was Barclays.

They then asked me a few programming questions, which I answered comfortably. After that, they asked if I knew anything about the stock market. I told them I had very limited knowledge and only understood that when people invest in businesses, both profits and losses depend on how the business performs.

They then asked about derivative instruments. Since I had no idea, they gave me a quick explanation of futures. After that, they asked me to write a program based on what they had just taught me. I wrote a simple program to predict future prices using a moving average, and they seemed quite positive about my performance.

They offered me the job on the spot and said I could join the following week. I made an excuse and said I could only join after a month, which they accepted. They then asked if I had my own laptop, explaining that as a startup with a tight budget, they wanted to avoid unnecessary expenses until they became profitable. I told them I didn’t have a personal laptop (it was shared with my siblings), so they said it was fine and that I could use one of theirs since it wasn’t in use 24/7.

Finally, they asked about my salary expectations. I said I was looking for a 40% hike on my current CTC, and they agreed immediately.

After the interview, I left. Two days later, I emailed them saying I had received another offer from a Japan based investment bank and would be joining them. Within an hour, they called and offered me an additional 1% equity on top of the CTC I had asked for. I politely declined.

text:
For me comments like this make all the effort worth it.

When we started In The Money by Zerodha, the vision was to create the kind of trading content we wished existed - practical, honest, contextual, and free from sneaky tips, product plugs and get-rich-quick promises.

Nearly 10 months later, we’ve published 100+ videos, 50+ Shorts, interviewed incredible traders and market mavens from around the world - From Tom Sosnoff, Rob Carver, Rob Hanna, Andreas Clenow, and many more, and somewhere along the way we crossed 75K+ subscribers.

The numbers are encouraging, but knowing we’ve helped people think differently about markets is what matters the most.

Thank you to everyone who has watched, shared, and supported us in this journey and the entire production team which puts more effort than one can imagine. 

In all I look at this as just the beginning.

text:
Something important I don’t think enough people are paying attention to with Variational is what’s coming over the next couple of weeks.

Three major upgrades are on the roadmap:

• TradFi Liquidity for RWAs
• Trading API
• Omni Liquidity Provider (OLP)

The Trading API should help drive more volume and open interest as more traders and automated trading systems connect to the platform.

TradFi Liquidity could improve spreads and overall trading conditions across listed RWAs, making the market more attractive to trade and driving even more volume and OI.

Then there’s OLP.

Most people think of it as another liquidity product, but it’s much more than that.

Because OLP is integrated directly into trade execution, additional trading volume actually makes the system more efficient rather than putting more strain on it. As volume increases, the marginal cost of hedging falls, allowing pricing to become even more competitive. (thanks @Mennodeg for the insight)

That creates a powerful flywheel:

More Volume → Cheaper Hedging → Better Spreads → More Traders → More Volume

Unlike many liquidity models, this doesn’t rely on external market makers or temporary incentives. The value compounds within the protocol itself.

Individually, each of these upgrades is important. Together, they strengthen the metrics that matter most: volume, open interest, TVL, liquidity, execution quality, and ultimately revenue.

Higher trading activity means more protocol revenue. More revenue means greater capacity for buybacks, a stronger treasury, and more resources to reinvest into the protocol both before and after TGE.

According to the current timeline, TradFi Liquidity is expected before the end of this month, followed by the Trading API, and then OLP.

If execution goes as planned, the next 1-2 months could mark a major inflection point for Variational.

Something to reflect on 👀 

Just getting started on Variational?
Use “OMNISAM” for 15% points boost (max possible)

text:
Last week I was in Bangalore for the Dhan Dev event, and I got to talk about how anyone can use AI to validate and backtest trading ideas without writing a single line of code themselves.

The core of my talk was simple. Most traders have ideas. But the gap between having an idea and knowing whether it actually works has always been huge. You need data, you need to code the logic, you need to run the numbers, and you need to do it honestly without fooling yourself. That gap is exactly where most people give up. They either trade the idea blindly or they never test it at all.

What I showed at the event is how that gap has basically collapsed.

Using Claude Code together with the Dhan MCP and Dhan skills, you can describe a trading idea in plain English and watch it turn into a working backtest.

Big thanks to the @DhanHQ team for putting this together and for building the kind of developer tooling that makes all of this possible. The MCP and skills approach is genuinely ahead of the curve for the Indian market.

text:
📜✨ VEDIC ASTROLOGY — THE BLUEPRINT OF YOUR LIFE ✨📜

Think astrology is just about zodiac signs?  
It’s actually a deep science that reads your entire life story through planets, nakshatras, houses & tithis 🌌🔮

---

🔥 WHAT MAKES VEDIC ASTROLOGY SO ACCURATE?  
It’s not just one thing — it’s a combination of 3 branches:
1️⃣ Siddhant → The math behind planets & panchang 🧮  
2️⃣ Samhita → Mundane astrology & natural events 🌦️  
3️⃣ Hora → The actual prediction of your life events 💫

Today we focus on HORA — how to read a birth chart accurately 👇

---

🌟 THE 5 KEY PILLARS OF PREDICTION:

🪐 GRAHAS (PLANETS) — 9 planets, each with their own nature & energy  
🌙 NAKSHATRAS — 27 (or 28) lunar mansions. The nakshatra lord shapes your personality & life path completely 🌠  
📅 TITHIS — 15 tithis in a lunar fortnight. Born on Dwitiya? You’ll be gentle. Tritiya? Physically & mentally sensitive. Each tithis carries a unique nature 🌕🌑  
🏠 BHAVAS (HOUSES) — 12 houses = 12 areas of life. Career, health, marriage, wealth, children — everything is mapped here 📍  
♈ RASHIS (SIGNS) — 12 zodiac signs with their own element & nature: Fire, Earth, Air, Water 🔥🌍💨💧

---

🧠 HOW IT ALL CONNECTS:
Moon’s nakshatra & phase → decides your birth name letter & Rashi 🌙
Day of birth → Monday = calm nature | Tuesday = fiery | Wednesday = intelligent but restless | Thursday = wise | Friday = glamorous | Saturday = detached | Rahu = ambitious | Ketu = spiritual 🧘‍♂️
Rashi nature → Is it Pitta (fire), Prithvi (earth), Vayu (air)? Is it Urdhva Mukhi (upward) or Adho Mukhi (downward)? This shapes your career path — doctor, officer, engineer, spiritual seeker 👨‍⚕️🪖

---

💡 PLANETARY PERIODS MATTER MOST  
Sun → 30 days in a sign | Moon → 2.25 days | Mars → 36-40 days | Mercury → 30-32 days | Jupiter → 1-1.5 years | Rahu/Ketu → 1.5 years each 🕰️
Dasha (planetary period) + Antar Dasha (sub-period) = the real timeline of what’s happening in your life right now ⏳

---

🏛️ THE 12 HOUSES = YOUR LIFE’S STORYBOOK
1️⃣ Lagna — Your personality & self  
2️⃣ Dhan — Wealth, family, speech  
3️⃣ Parakram — Courage, effort, siblings  
4️⃣ Sukha — Mother, home, peace, heart health  
5️⃣ Vidya — Education, intelligence, children, love life  
6️⃣ Roga — Diseases, enemies, competition, strength  
…and so on till the 12th house of spirituality & liberation 🕉️

Each house + planet + Rashi = a whole chapter of your life 📖

---

🔮 THE POWER OF PLANETARY YUTIS & DRSHTI
Sun + Rahu in Gemini → Obstacles in government jobs & business 💼🚫
Saturn in 8th → Long life but long illness periods; protects from death in accidents 🛡️
Ketu = 4X power. Can destroy or make you a brand — depends if it’s auspicious or malefic 🌟

---

💎 GRAHA BAL — PLANETARY STRENGTH
Mool Trikona → 80-85% strength 💪  
Swa Rashi → 60-65%  
Mitra Rashi → 50-60%  
Shatru Rashi → 30-40%  
Your planet behaves differently based on where it sits 🏠

---

🚨 THE REAL TRUTH ABOUT REMEDIES
Color therapy, placing steel glasses, or changing wall paint? Mostly myth 🚫  
Your 7 planets already carry their own colors from birth. Change your karma, not just your walls 🧘‍♂️✨

---

🎓 WHY LEARN THIS?  
With this knowledge, you can spot:  
👶 Childbirth issues | 🧠 Mental health | 💔 Marriage troubles | 💰 Financial blocks | 🦴 Health problems — just by looking at the chart 👁️

---

💬 Ready to dive deeper?  
This is just the BASIC. In advanced classes we cover D9, D10, D60 charts, yogas, doshas, gemstones, vastu, face reading & more 🔥

Trading bots are easy to sketch. The hard part is keeping backtests and live execution honest.

AlgoTrader is a Claude Code skill for builders working on quantitative trading systems for Indian equity markets with Zerodha/Kite workflows.

It helps you move from idea to a safer implementation by combining a bot-generation wizard, NSE universe fetching, backtest/live checks, and a documented set of trading-system gotchas.

Key features:

• Interactive bot wizard – scans existing code and asks about trade style, universe, capital, and risk tolerance
• Zerodha/Kite workflow guidance – covers tick-size rounding, position reconciliation, stop-loss lifecycle, and credentials setup
• Live universe fetcher – creates JSON files for Nifty 50, Nifty 100, Midcap 150, and Smallcap 250 constituents
• Backtest-live parity checks – documents data caching, VWAP reset, candle completion, and slippage issues that break live results
• Risk and analytics patterns – includes Kelly sizing, portfolio heat, regime detection, structured logs, and post-trade reporting

It’s open-source (MIT license).

query: (#FnO OR #optionstrading OR #optionselling OR #optionbuying OR "option trading") min_faves:15 lang:en since:2026-06-22 until:2026-06-30
text:
BTC vol update:
• Atm IV is ~40%, the yearly average
• 25Δ put skew has risen to an all-year high

This skew is what you get when options traders are very comfortable selling upside calls to buy downside puts.

It's the options market saying:

(1) MSTR buying will slow down and can't push price higher, whereas any signs of stress in prices of STRC and MSTR, can weigh on BTC disproportionally.

(2) BTC in the 60s has given plenty of chance for longs to position whether ETFs, whales or funds but the perception of it being great value is diminishing.

Opportunity set to consider.

(1) Sell otm put spreads -- short skew trade

As Sinclair highlights in Positional Option Trading:

If a trader wants to collect the skew premium, the safest way is to sell a put spread rather than possibly offset this by purchasing a call spread. The long put will almost certainly be the option with the highest implied volatility in the structure, but because the short put will still be trading at a volatility premium to the ATM this position is still short implied skew.

For this trade, timing matters. The trade is most attractive when BTC is falling toward support at 60, or goes even lower, and the position feels uncomfortable to enter. That is when the market is paying the most for downside insurance.

The lower strike put you buy will be expensive, but you do need to cap your downside, as price re-discovery in the 50s is not safe territory for naked short puts.

(2) Sell hedged straddles -- IV vs RV trade

If we stay in range, heading into summer, which is the seasonal low vol point, then implied at 40 will likely exceed realized.

If BTC breaks down, you can be fine if it's a clean break into the 50s. Hedging short gamma in a choppy range is expensive. Doing so in a down trend is cheap, might even be profitable, especially if price settles down far enough from the strike where the structure has low residual gamma.

The bad case would be a V-reversal back into 60s. That'd be high realized hedging cost against the implied you sold.

author: optionstraddler
query: (#algotrading OR "algo trading" OR kiteconnect OR "kite api" OR openalgo OR "dhan api" OR "trading api") min_faves:5 lang:en since:2026-06-22 until:2026-06-30
text:
This isn't a get-rich post. It's a "here's what discipline actually pays" post.

Real capital, real rules, a verified P&L — and the truth most people won't tell you about trading small.

Let me be specific about what this is, because vague flexing helps no one. This is equity intraday. Cash market. Buy and sell the same stock the same day, every position squared off before the close. Nothing held overnight. No options, no positional bets, no waiting weeks for a thesis to play out. Just clean, same-day execution — done by an algo, not by my mood.

I'd never seriously traded this way before. I come from derivatives. So on November 1, 2025, I did something uncomfortable: I handed my equity intraday system real money and a strict set of rules, and I promised myself I wouldn't interfere. Not once. Same algo logic. Same risk discipline I built for derivatives. No new opinions, no gut calls.

Here's the honest setup, because numbers without context are just bragging:

Capital actually put to work — ₹50,000.
The broker gives 5X intraday leverage on top of that. So ₹50k can move up to roughly ₹2.5L worth of stock — but only intraday. Everything gets squared off the same day. No overnight gaps, no surprise news risk while I sleep. The leverage lives and dies inside market hours.

And here's the part most people skip: I keep ₹75,000 parked in the account. That extra cushion isn't there to take bigger positions. It's there for drawdown. It's the buffer that lets the algo survive a rough patch without me panicking and breaking my own system.

That buffer matters more than the profit. A strategy that can't survive its bad days is just gambling with extra steps.
Now the result — Nov 1, 2025 to today:
Net realised P&L: ₹1,12,496
Most profitable single day: ₹9,691
Longest green streak: 6 days
Brokerage saved: ₹6,398

Verified P&L. Not a screenshot I drew in Paint.
https://t.co/hJ61Jgc2g7

And remember — this isn't one lucky trade. Intraday means many small, repeatable trades. The edge isn't a hero call; it's the same boring decision made correctly, hundreds of times, without emotion.
Yes, the percentage looks loud against ₹50k. I'm deliberately not shouting it, because that's exactly where people get fooled.

There are two myths floating around this market:

Myth 1 — "Small capital, you can get rich."

Myth 2 — "Small capital, you can't get any results."

Both are wrong.
You won't get rich from a small account. Anyone selling you that dream is selling you something — usually a course. But you absolutely can get real, meaningful, compounding results — if the process behind it is real.

The middle truth is the boring one nobody posts about: small capital + discipline + a tested edge = steady results. Not a lottery ticket. A skill that scales as the capital grows.

So what actually produced these numbers? Not luck. Not a tip. Three unglamorous things:

One — risk-reward was fixed before every single trade. The algo knew its exit before its entry. Loss capped, target defined, no "let me just give it some room" nonsense.

Two — the strategy was backtested and researched until I trusted the data more than I trusted my own opinion. If it didn't survive the testing, it didn't make it to live money.

Three — my expectations were realistic. I wasn't trying to double the account in a week. I was trying not to break the rules in a bad week. That's a completely different goal — and it's the one that actually pays.

That's the whole game. Risk-reward, backtested research, realistic expectations. Everything else is noise.

Algo trading didn't hand me a money machine. It handed me consistency — and it removed the one thing that used to cost me the most: my own emotions. The machine doesn't revenge-trade. It doesn't get greedy on a green day or scared on a red one. It just executes.

The market doesn't reward the smartest opinion. It rewards the person who follows a tested process when it's boring, and especially when it hurts.

Small capital isn't the limitation everyone thinks it is. The process is the limitation — or the edge. Yours to decide which.

PNL Attached. Equity Intraday — Cash Market, with Algo. Capital deployed ₹50,000, buffer maintained ₹75,000.

text:
I Used to Only Sell Options

For years, I was an option seller. That was my game. That was my edge.

Option buying? Not a fan. I trusted selling, and I let my system do only that.

Then I went deeper into algo trading.

When you build algos, you stop trading on opinion. You test. You let the data speak — not your bias.
And the data didn't care what I preferred.

It showed me that selling worked well in some market conditions. And in other conditions, buying setups were the ones that performed. Different markets. Different tools.

I had been forcing one style onto every situation — because I liked it, not because it was always right.
So I stopped being loyal to a side. I built and tested buying strategies the same way I built my selling ones.

Same discipline. Same backtesting. Same risk rules.

I kept what survived the data. I dropped what didn't.
Now my algos do both. They sell when the conditions favor selling. They buy when the conditions favor buying. The machine doesn't have an ego. It just follows what works.

That's the part that changed me.

Algo trading didn't make me a better seller or a better buyer. It made me neutral. And neutrality is the edge.
I used to be an option seller who avoided buying. Now I'm just a trader who lets the data decide.

PNL Attached Option Buying with Algo Capital Used 2 Lakhs.

NSDL vs CDSL — 

The Hidden Battle Behind India's ₹600+ Lakh Crore Demat Ecosystem 📊

India's demat revolution is one of the biggest wealth-creation stories of the decade.

But beneath the surface, two giants are fighting very different battles:

🟢 CDSL = Retail Dominance
🔵 NSDL = Institutional Dominance

🇮🇳 The Numbers (May 2026)

✅ Total Demat Accounts: 22.9+ Crore

📈 CDSL

 • 18.38 Crore accounts
• ~80% market share by accounts
• Preferred by Zerodha, Groww, Upstox and retail investors
• Biggest beneficiary of India's retail investing boom

🏦 NSDL 

• 4.51 Crore accounts
• ~20% market share by accounts
• Preferred by FIIs, Mutual Funds, Banks & HNIs
• Dominates high-value custody assets

The Real Story

While CDSL owns the customers, NSDL owns the capital.

💰 NSDL controls over 86% of India's demat custody value, despite having only ~20% of accounts. 

This means:

👉 Retail investors are opening accounts at CDSL at a massive pace.
👉 Institutions continue to trust NSDL for large-value holdings.
👉 Both are direct beneficiaries of India's financialization trend.

Demat Boom in Numbers

📊 2022: ~9 Crore Demat Accounts
📊 2026: ~22.9 Crore Demat Accounts

That's one of the fastest retail participation expansions in global market history. 

Investor Takeaway

🔹 CDSL wins on volume growth
🔹 NSDL wins on asset value & institutional trust
🔹 Both enjoy strong network effects and high entry barriers

The real winner?

📈 India's capital market ecosystem.

As millions of Indians shift from savings to investing, depositories may remain among the biggest long-term beneficiaries.

If you had to own only one for the next 10 years — which would you choose?

text:
A few people asked how I manage swing trades and long-term holdings in the 𝘀𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗰𝗸 when holding both in the 𝘀𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁.

I use 𝘁𝘄𝗼 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁𝘀 with Zerodha.

→ 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝗗𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁: For accumulation and swing trades 
→ 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝗗𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁: For long-term dividend holdings

When a stock reaches my target quantity (say 100 shares), I transfer those shares to the secondary demat account.

From that point, I treat them as long-term holdings.

The best part? 😂

I can't sell them immediately.

To sell, I first need to transfer them back to the primary account and then place the sell order.

That extra step gives me time to think and helps prevent emotional decisions.

This setup helps me:
→ Separate trading capital from long-term investments 
→ Stay focused on dividend accumulation 
→ Avoid mixing trading and investing decisions 
→ Build positions with a long-term mindset

It's not about avoiding FIFO. It's about creating a system that matches my investing style.

→ One account for trading.
→ One account for dividends.
